EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of relative humidity values below 25 percent and wind gusts up to 30 mph created Red Flag Warning conditions ahead of an approaching cold front. A significant wildfire developed in Berkeley County in the afternoon hours, in addition to several small grass and brush fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Berkeley County Emergency Management reported a wildfire near Red Hill Road and Havens Ridge Place. The fire was estimated to be approximately 100 acres in size and one shed was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty gradient winds spread across southeast South Carolina ahead of an approaching cold front. Frequent wind gusts of 40-50 mph were observed, including a peak wind gust of 55 mph at the Charleston International Airport (KCHS). The gusty winds resulted in a few downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colleton County Emergency Management reported a tree down along Interstate 95 near mile marker 41 blocking one lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued dry and windy conditions lead to relative humidities falling below 20% and Red Flag criteria conditions in the afternoon of March 26th. A brush grew in Aiken grew rapidly and forced the evacuations of 25 homes in Aiken as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aiken County EM Director reports there is a large brush fire at the vicinity of Glass Rope Lane and New Holland Road. The fire grew to at least 60 acres in the afternoon. 25 homes were evacuated as a result. The fire was 100% contained by 8am March 27th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 30-foot tall trees blown down on Breezewood Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad mid-level trough pushed across the eastern CONUS throughout January 10th. The associated surface low pressure and warm advection caused a brief band of heavy snow, sleet, and freezing rain to develop in the Central Savannah River Area and southern Midlands. As temperatures warmed aloft and cold air damming conditions strengthened at the surface, the freezing rain shifted northwards with portions of the central and northern Midlands receiving up to 0.25 ice overnight into January 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow mixed with sleet moved into the county on the morning of the 10th. A COOP observer estimated around 1.5 inches of snow and sleet accumulated in McCormick. Warm air moved in aloft and by the afternoon, snow and sleet transitioned to freezing rain but no ice accumulation was reported. By late afternoon, temperatures rose above freezing and precipitation transitioned to liquid rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and lift associated with low pressure developing along the Gulf Coast overspread the Southeast and combined with cold surface high pressure to result in development of snow across upstate South Carolina during the late morning. Snow steadily accumulated, with amounts of around an inch reported by mid-afternoon. Precipitation then began to transition to sleet and freezing rain as temperatures warmed aloft. By early evening, ice accretion of around 1/4 inch was reported across much of the area, with isolated to scattered power outages reported.

A broad mid-level trough pushed across the eastern CONUS throughout January 10th. The associated surface low pressure and warm advection caused a brief band of heavy snow, sleet, and freezing rain to develop in the Central Savannah River Area and southern Midlands. As temperatures warmed aloft and cold air damming conditions strengthened at the surface, the freezing rain shifted northwards with portions of the central and northern Midlands receiving up to 0.25 ice overnight into January 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief band of heavy snow mixed with sleet moved into the county on the morning of the 10th. A COOP observer in North Augusta measured 1 inch of snow mixed with sleet. A COOP observer in Aiken also measured 1 inch of snow/sleet. This impacted the morning commute with several accidents and road closures reported, especially in and around the city of Aiken. Warm air moved in aloft and precipitation transitioned to freezing rain by mid to late morning. Freezing rain accumulated on trees and power lines, which an NWS employee reported was around a tenth of an inch along I-20. South Carolina Highway Patrol reported several downed tree limbs, likely due to the weight of ice accumulation. By evening, temperatures at the surface warmed to above freezing and any lingering precipitation transitioned to liquid rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved into the southeastern US with a developing surface low moving near the Georgia and South Carolina coast. Light snow began in the morning but as warm advection and frontogenesis strengthened, a band of moderate to heavy snow developed by the evening of the 21st, continuing into the overnight with amounts exceeding 2 inches in the southeastern Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow moved into the area by mid afternoon with a brief period of moderate snow late in the evening. Snowfall accumulations were generally between a half inch and one and a half inches. The highest report of 1.8 inches came from the county emergency manager just southwest of Cheraw.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began early in the afternoon with a moderate band moving into the area by the evening into the overnight. Snow accumulations were generally between 1 to 3 inches with the highest observed snowfall of 2.5 inches west of I-26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rare November supercells developed across South Carolina as a deep mid and upper level trough pushed across the southeast US. This trough interacted with anomalously high instability and a steep moisture gradient that helped enhance the wind fields to favor supercellular convection. Each of these supercells produced large hail and damaging winds during their lifetime in the central and western Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of hail around quarter sized near Wickers Greenhouse along Highway 76. Time estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newberry Dispatch reported pine trees down over Floyd Rd near the intersection with Bel Ivy Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad mid-level trough pushed across the eastern CONUS throughout January 10th. The associated surface low pressure and warm advection caused a brief band of heavy snow, sleet, and freezing rain to develop in the Central Savannah River Area and southern Midlands. As temperatures warmed aloft and cold air damming conditions strengthened at the surface, the freezing rain shifted northwards with portions of the central and northern Midlands receiving up to 0.25 ice overnight into January 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of mostly sleet moved into the area on the morning of the 10th. A spotter just west of Orangeburg measured 0.3 of sleet accumulation. As temperatures aloft warmed, sleet transitioned to freezing rain. The Orangeburg County Emergency Manager reported radial ice accumulation around 0.15 and the OGB ASOS reported 0.14. The EM reported notable ice accumulations on trees and elevated surfaces. By late afternoon, temperatures warmed above freezing and any lingering precipitation fell as liquid rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the morning with bands of moderate to heavy snow moving in at times during the evening and into the overnight. Widespread accumulation amounts of 1 to 3 inches with the highest amounts in the southern and southeastern portions of the county. The highest accumulation observed was 3 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer between Jackson and Beech Island.

Snow accumulations were generally between 3 to 5 inches. Two separate reports of 5.0 inches came from southeast of Branchville and just north of Eutawville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and lift associated with low pressure developing along the Gulf Coast overspread the Southeast and combined with cold surface high pressure to result in development of snow and sleet across the South Carolina Piedmont during early afternoon. The precipitation gradually accumulated, with half inch to an inch of sleet and snow reported across the area by early evening. At that point, warming temperatures aloft resulted in a transition to freezing rain, which persisted through the evening before tapering off during the early morning. Total ice accretion of around 1/4 inch was reported on top of the sleet and snow.

A prolonged period of widespread precipitation fell across southeast South Carolina in the presence of an arctic airmass, beginning in the late afternoon hours and continuing after sunrise the following morning. The setup resulted in a rare winter storm across the region, with all of southeast South Carolina being impacted by significant accumulations of snow and sleet. Temperatures remained cold for several days following the end of the winter weather, so impacts continued for an extended period of time. Hazardous travel conditions persisted for several days as well, causing significant disruptions across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rare winter storm produced significant accumulations of snow and sleet across Allendale County. Storm total snow/sleet accumulations include public reports of 3.0 inches in Allendale and 3.5 inches in Fairfax.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad mid-level trough pushed across the eastern CONUS throughout January 10th. The associated surface low pressure and warm advection caused a brief band of heavy snow, sleet, and freezing rain to develop in the Central Savannah River Area and southern Midlands. As temperatures warmed aloft and cold air damming conditions strengthened at the surface, the freezing rain shifted northwards with portions of the central and northern Midlands receiving up to 0.25 ice overnight into January 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of mostly sleet mixed with snow moved into the county on the morning of the 10th, although there were no reports of accumulation. As temperatures warmed aloft, any sleet or snow transitioned to freezing rain by late morning into the afternoon. The Columbia Jim Hamilton L.B. Owens ASOS (KCUB) measured 0.21 of ice accumulation. South Carolina Highway Patrol reported two trees fell in the NW portion of the county, likely due to the weight of ice on limbs. By the night of the 10th, surface temperatures warmed to above freezing and any lingering precipitation transitioned to liquid rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and lift associated with low pressure developing along the Gulf Coast overspread the Southeast and combined with cold surface high pressure to result in development of snow and sleet across the South Carolina Piedmont during early afternoon. The precipitation gradually accumulated, with half inch to an inch of sleet and snow reported across the area by early evening. At that point, warming temperatures aloft resulted in a transition to freezing rain, which persisted through the evening before tapering off during the early morning. Total ice accretion of around 1/4 inch was reported on top of the sleet and snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rare winter storm produced significant accumulations of snow and sleet across Charleston County. Storm total snow/sleet accumulations of 2.0-3.5 inches were observed across the county. The highest totals were generally found in around North Charleston, Goose Creek, and Hanahan due to slightly higher amounts of snow relative to other parts of the county. Elsewhere, sleet was the predominant precipitation type which yielded lower storm totals despite similar liquid precipitation amounts. Some of the highest totals include a public report of 3.5 inches on Wadmalaw Island, 4.0 inches near Goose Creek, a CoCoRaHS observation of 3.5 inches near Park Circle, and 4.0 inches near Hanahan. Also of note, 2.5 inches of storm total snow/sleet was measured by National Weather Service personnel at the Charleston International Airport (KCHS) which ranks as the 6th biggest winter storm on record. Climate records at KCHS date back to March 1st, 1937.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light precipitation developed just after sunrise and spread into portions of southeast South Carolina through the early afternoon hours. As the precipitation moved in, temperatures across the area were below freezing. Evaporative cooling processes allowed for precipitation to fall as a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain across portions of Colleton, Dorchester, Berkeley, and Charleston counties. Some areas received light accumulations which yielded hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of sleet and freezing rain fell across mostly the northern portion of Colleton County. The winter weather caused some travel disruption including ice on the roadway on Highway 61 near Happiness Lane, resulting in a vehicle collision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light precipitation developed just after sunrise and spread into portions of southeast South Carolina through the early afternoon hours. Evaporative cooling processes allowed for precipitation to fall as a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain across portions of Colleton, Dorchester, Berkeley, and Charleston counties. Some areas received light accumulations which yielded hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light amounts of sleet and freezing rain impacted much of Dorchester County. Measured amounts ranged from trace accumulations of freezing rain on roads, bridges, cars, trees, and untreated surfaces to two tenths of an inch of sleet near the intersection of Highway 78 and Highway 178. Numerous roads were impacted and hazardous to travel, including major roadways such as Interstate 95, Interstate 26, Highway 78, Highway 178, and Highway 15. Slick roadways and hazardous travel conditions resulted in multiple vehicle accidents in the county. One accident occurred on Shuman Drive and resulted in one reported injury. Two other accidents occurred on Interstate 26 near mile marker 182 and resulted in one reported injury.

Also, local broadcast media shared a picture of two tenths of an inch of sleet accumulation near Pinopolis on the south side of Lake Moultrie. Numerous roads were impacted and hazardous to travel, resulting in multiple vehicle accidents across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved into the southeastern US with a developing surface low moving near the Georgia and South Carolina coast. Light snow began in the morning but as warm advection and frontogenesis strengthened, a band of moderate to heavy snow developed by the evening of the 21st, continuing into the overnight with amounts exceeding 2 inches in the southeastern Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the early afternoon with a moderate to heavy band pushing in the evening and into the overnight. Snow tapered off early in the morning of the 22nd. Snow accumulation reports were between 2 to 4 inches with the highest amount of 3.5 inches reported by a COOP observer east of Barnwell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the early afternoon with a band of moderate to heavy snow moving in during the evening and continuing into the overnight. Snow totals were around 2 inches with two reports east of North of 2.2 inches from a spotter and from a CoCoRaHS observer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the early afternoon with a band of moderate to heavy snow moving in during the evening and continuing into the overnight. Snow accumulations were generally between 2 to 3 inches from a mix of public, spotter and CoCoRaHS reports. The highest reported total was 3.4 inches, just west of Cope.

Strong northwest winds in the wake of another arctic cold front reinforced very cold air over the South Carolina mountains during the early morning hours of the 22nd. Temperatures in the single digits and teens and winds gusting from 15 to 25 mph resulted in minimum wind chill values of 0 to 10 during this time. Stronger winds and temperatures near 0 caused wind chills of -5 or colder above 3500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushing through the region during late morning hours helped produce a line of showers with embedded thunderstorms capable of producing damaging wind gusts across Southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down on Hudson Mill Road between Norris Drive and Red Root Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable mixing into strong low-level wind fields ahead of a cold front led to gusty winds and low relative humidity values supportive for wildfires across Southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The United States Forest Service reported a wildfire between 75 to 100 acres large in the Francis Marion National Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough and associated cold front pushed the region during the morning of February 16th causing scattered wind damage as result of both gradient and severe thunderstorm winds. The line of heavy showers and thunderstorms was preceded by extremely impressive winds both aloft and near the surface. While instability remained limited, it was sufficient to produce a thin line of thunderstorms which mixed down damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Hwy Patrol reported trees down on I-77 south of Heritage Rd.

While instability remained limited, it was sufficient to produce a thin line of thunderstorms which mixed down damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aiken County Dispatch reported trees down on I-20 at Mile Marker 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation overspread the South Carolina mountains during the evening of the 11th in association with an approaching frontal system. While temperatures were above freezing across much of the area, a pocket of cold air developed along the Blue Ridge escarpment, resulting in precipitation falling as freezing rain there for a prolonged period. Total ice accretion was mainly in around 0.10 across this area. Meanwhile, the lower elevations closer to Highway 11 saw only rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and storm clusters developed across upstate South Carolina during the evening. One storm produced a brief tornado in Abbeville County. Storms and clusters tended to move slowly, resulting in locally heavy rainfall, with isolated flash flooding developing where multiple storms moved over the same areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported flash flooding developed in the Laurens area and in Waterloo after a swath of 3 to 4 inches of rain fell in this area in 2-3 hours. Long Lick Branch overflowed its banks in the Waterloo area and inundated Todd Quarter Rd, which had to be closed. Harris Springs Rd was also inundated by water from Cane Creek. In the city of Laurens, the Little River overflowed its banks and flooded Little River Park. A tributary of the Little River flooded a portion of Fleming St, where a motorist became stranded in floodwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough approached the region with an accompanying cold front on March 31st. Preceding the front, the forecast environment featured moderate instability and strengthening wind shear. Some bowing segments within a broken line of convection in the afternoon produced damaging winds and a supercell developed just ahead of the main line in eastern Orangeburg County which produced very large hail (up to 1.75 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Department of Public Safety reported a downed tree along Frank Shealy Road near Rocky Retreat Court.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wednesday March 5th saw a sharp mid level trough over the Great Lakes region with an associated surface low here as well. This low pressure system brought a strong cold front and a line of convection ahead of it during the morning hours.  A strong low level jet came into place across the region with 60-70 kts of southerly flow. As this line moved into the forecast area, strong non-thunderstorm gusts were reported ahead of the line with the strong low level winds mixing down to the surface. Once the convective line entered the area shortly after daybreak, little instability was seen but strong shear and the strong cold front drove the line before it exited by the early afternoon. This line resulted in numerous reports of thunderstorm related wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company reported a power outage caused by a downed tree on Beaver Creek Rd. near Hwy. 378.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public and utility company reported multiple trees blown down across southern Anderson County, from Iva to the Level Land community, and especially in Honea Path, where some trees fell on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough approached the region with an accompanying cold front on March 31st. Preceding the front, the forecast environment featured moderate instability and strengthening wind shear. Some bowing segments within a broken line of convection in the afternoon produced damaging winds and a supercell developed just ahead of the main line in eastern Orangeburg County which produced very large hail (up to 1.75 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Department of Public Safety reported trees and powerlines down at Vance Road and Easterlin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wednesday March 5th saw a sharp mid level trough over the Great Lakes region with an associated surface low here as well. This low pressure system brought a strong cold front and a line of convection ahead of it during the morning hours.  A strong low level jet came into place across the region with 60-70 kts of southerly flow. As this line moved into the forecast area, strong non-thunderstorm gusts were reported ahead of the line with the strong low level winds mixing down to the surface. Once the convective line entered the area shortly after daybreak, little instability was seen but strong shear and the strong cold front drove the line before it exited by the early afternoon. This line resulted in numerous reports of thunderstorm related wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a picture of a roof covering blown off at City Gas and Fuel. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low drifted into Great Lakes region with a cold front and line of convection nearing the forecast area by the early morning hours. Very strong shear dominated throughout the event with instability rather meager. The line of convection moved across the area from the west between 11z and 18-19z, bringing multiple reports of straight line wind damage, an EF-0 tornado in Barnwell County and an EF-1 tornado near Lake Murray in Lexington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF-0 tornado touched down between Snelling and Barnwell, SC with peak winds of 80 mph. The short-lived tornado began in the Sweetwater Country Club, between Fairway Lane and Club House Lane, and moved to the northeast. The tornado uprooted at least 5 large pine trees and snapped several large branches before dissipating around Dunbarton Blvd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team determined a tornado formed over Lake Murray near Dreher Island before causing EF0 and low end EF1 damage over the Timberlake community. The tornado continued northeast toward Isle of Pines before re-entering the lake, turning more ENE. It caused more EF0 damage from Indian Cove Rd to Wessinger Rd. The tornado then dissipated before reaching Indian Fork Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team confirmed a near continuous swath of straight line wind damage beginning near Swansea, extending southeast across Lake Marion into Clarendon County. An EF1 tornado also occurred within this broader swath of damage. Combining ground observations and radar, this swath started with a strong macroburst near Swansea causing substantial tree damage along Pine Plain Road and elsewhere. The damaging winds pushed southeast across Saint Matthews and Lone Star before crossing Lake Marion. Additional damaging winds occurred in Summerton, Davis Station, and Wyboo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Southeast region during the afternoon on May 30th with an associated surface cold front. These features moved into an environment with modest instability and strong deep layer shear, yielding a severe line of convection that brought wind damage and a tornado south of Manning, SC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey team confirmed an EF1 tornado in Clarendon County that occurred within a wide swath of damaging winds, extending from Swansea to Lake Marion. Based on ground observations and radar, the tornado likely developed over Lake Marion, just east of Lone Star before emerging over land near Hickory Top Road. The tornado snapped numerous limbs and large branches along Old River Road as it moved southeast. The tornado strengthened and caused substantial tree and power line damage near Old River Road and Clifton Road and then further east near Liberty Hill Road and Rapid Edge Trail. Moving southeast, minor tree damage occurred across Highway 15 and I-95. Numerous trees were then uprooted along MG Hemingway Road. The tornado slowly weakened causing minor damage across Washington Davis Road before dissipating near Bill Davis Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low slowly moved into the Tennessee Valley with an associated surface cold front moving into the area during the late afternoon and into the evening. This sparked scattered convection in a modestly unstable environment, but solid deep layer shear and mid-level lapse rates yielded severe hail in Bamberg County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Facebook post with a video of hail falling on Low Country Hwy between Olar and Ehrhardt. Additional photo showed a hailstone that was approximately quarter size. Time provided by user and confirmed by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high-end EF-1 tornado struck the community of Langley, SC overnight on May 12th. The tornado was embedded within an area of heavy rain in an otherwise unfavorable environment to severe weather across the area. It was the only severe report of the event other than some minor flash flooding that was reported later that morning across northern Orangeburg county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey team confirmed a brief high-end EF-1 tornado with winds up to 105 mph in Langley, SC that occurred around 330 am. The tornado began near Pinecrest Drive snapping several large branches before uprooting numerous trees in Langley Cemetery. Moving northwest, the tornado snapped numerous trees and caused minor roof damage to multiple homes between Piney House Road and Chaffee Street. After crossing near Cushman Road, the tornado caused substantial tree, siding, and shingle damage between Piney Heights Road and Dunbar Street. The fire department and adjacent structures along Augusta Road then sustained high-end EF-1 damage with garage door failure, outbuildings destroyed, and the metal roofing ripped off and thrown several hundred yards. Additional tree damage occurred along Langley Dam Road with dozens of uprooted trees before dissipating near Jefferson Davis Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and storm clusters developed across upstate South Carolina during the evening. One storm produced a brief tornado in Abbeville County. Storms and clusters tended to move slowly, resulting in locally heavy rainfall, with isolated flash flooding developing where multiple storms moved over the same areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported flash flooding developed across the north side of the city of Greenwood after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell over the city in only 2-3 hours. The main impact was to Rocky Creek, which overflowed its banks and inundated portions of Haltiwanger Rd and Rockcreek Blvd. An apartment complex on Haltiwanger Rd was evacuated after bring threatened by floodwater. Multiple roads were reportedly closed throughout the city due to a combination of urban flash flooding and small stream inundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A largely unstable and weakly sheared environment developing ahead of shortwave energy approaching the region supported severe thunderstorms capable of producing damaging wind gusts across Southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Dorchester County emergency manager reported a tree down on a power line in the 100 Block of Pendarvis Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rare November supercells developed across South Carolina as a deep mid and upper level trough pushed across the southeast US. This trough interacted with anomalously high instability and a steep moisture gradient that helped enhance the wind fields to favor supercellular convection. Each of these supercells produced large hail and damaging winds during their lifetime in the central and western Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newberry County EM sent pictures of trees snapped & uprooted along Peak Rd. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer moisture in place with convergence along a surface trough led to widespread showers and thunderstorms with moderate destabilization during the afternoon. An isolated microburst produced damage in Saluda County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saluda County emergency manager reported a couple chicken houses were destroyed and tin from the roof of a building near Simmons Rd was lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper ridging in place to the west of the area, over the central US. A MCS developed east of the ridge axis and moved through the Deep South. Deep layer moisture in place and convergence from colliding outflow boundaries led to heavy rain. This caused urban flash flooding during the night of August 15th into the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbia Fire Dept responded to the intersection of Lamar and Kortright streets where a vehicle was submerged in floodwaters and one person had to be rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a highly unstable environment along a lingering boundary from a MCS that moved through the night before. Weak flow led to slow moving storms, propagating slowly along an outflow boundary. With precipitable water exceeding 2 inches and high soil moisture, this led to flash flooding near the Savannah River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aiken County Emergency Manager reported flash flooding on Howlandville Rd near Pine Log Rd.

This led to isolated flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rare November supercells developed across South Carolina as a deep mid and upper level trough pushed across the southeast US. This trough interacted with anomalously high instability and a steep moisture gradient that helped enhance the wind fields to favor supercellular convection. Each of these supercells produced large hail and damaging winds during their lifetime in the central and western Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey team surveyed damage off of Peak Rd in Pomaria in Newberry County where a strong supercell moved through the evening of November 8th, 2025. The survey found multiple large cedar trees that were either snapped or fully uprooted, mainly facing south or southeast in a divergent pattern. There was also extensive siding damage to the north side of a mobile home from wind-driven hail which was up to golf ball sized. The survey along with radar analysis determined that the wind damage was consistent with a strong downburst within the forward flank downdraft of the supercell with winds up to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approached the coast Wednesday afternoon. At the surface a stalling cold front intersected the sea breeze boundary while a moderately unstable airmass and steep lapse rates aloft combined to produce thunderstorms with large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms developed over the South Carolina Piedmont in association with a surface low and stalled frontal boundary draped over the Coastal Plain. Localized very heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding across portions of central and southern York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flash flooding developed across central and southern York County after 3.5 to 5.5 inches of rain fell in just a few hours. Inundation of Wilson Chapel Rd occurred in the Sharon area, likely due to Bullock Creek overflowing its banks. One lane of Bookout Rd in McConnells was flooded by water from South Fork Fishing Creek. A tributary of Stony Fork flooded Stallcup Rd just southwest of Rock Hill. Phil Grant Rd was washed out near Strait Rd near the Chester County line due to a small drainage. Additionally, extensive yard flooding was reported in a neighborhood on the north side of Rock Hill due to Big Dutchman Creek overflowing its banks. However, other than water entering a couple of outbuildings, no structures or roads were impacted in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low drifted into Great Lakes region with a cold front and line of convection nearing the forecast area by the early morning hours. Very strong shear dominated throughout the event with instability rather meager. The line of convection moved across the area from the west between 11z and 18-19z, bringing multiple reports of straight line wind damage, an EF-0 tornado in Barnwell County and an EF-1 tornado near Lake Murray in Lexington County.

Continuing|northeast, the tornado produced minor tree damage as it crossed|Amicks Ferry Road. Further east, substantial tree damage was found|on Lake Tide Drive with several dozen uprooted or snapped trees.|Additional tree damage was found across Sandbar Road and Lazy Creek|Court on the adjacent peninsulas. Numerous snapped limbs and debris|were found around Crooked Creek Park and Old Lexington Highway and|additional EF-1 damage behind Northshore Drive. The tornado crossed|Highway 76 causing a mix of EF-0 and EF-1 tree damage along Crooked|Creek Road and minor housing damage along Hollyoak Lane. The tornado|then lifted northeast of Hollyoak Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same slow moving long wave trough caused severe weather on preceding days helped trigger and organize another broken line of severe convection in the Midlands of SC on June 9th. Sufficient low level shear and strong instability helped produce two tornadoes along with a swath of scattered damaging winds into the central Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Greenville-Spartanburg storm survey confirmed a brief tornado touched down in Bobby Brown State Park in Elbert County, downing trees across Bobby Brown State Park Road and intensifying as it crossed Ranger Road. Dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted as the tornado approached a cove along the Savannah River. Some structural damage to pavilions occurred at the Bobby Brown Recreational Center. Tree damage was observed across the river in McCormick County SC, with some uprooted trees and large limbs broken. It is unknown how far into McCormick County the tornado may have traveled due to lack of roads on US Army Corps of Engineers property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A digging trough with strong mid-level winds helped trigger and develop a well organized mesoscale convective system across the southeast US on June 7th. Robust low level moisture and dry air aloft produced an ideal environment for strong instability to mix down strong winds to the surface. This resulted in a wide and long lived swath of damaging straight line winds throughout western and central SC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McCormick County Dispatch reported trees down throughout the county. Time Estimated  by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe storms developed as a mid level disturbance moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WMBF news posted pictures of trees down along Kings Highway between 29th Ave South and Farrow Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe storms developed as a mid level disturbance moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Horry County Fire Department responded to a shed set on fire by lightning on Hayloft Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds along the coast combined with increasing swell energy resulted in an enhanced risk of rip currents along the southeast South Carolina coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaufort County Emergency Management reported that a one person drowned and several others were rescued from a rip current at Hunting Island State Park beach. The rip current occurred in front of the Hunting Island lighthouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours inland of the sea breeze across portions of southeast South Carolina. These storms produced gusty winds and frequent cloud to ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colleton County Emergency Management reported a tree down on Pine Grove Road near the intersection with Benton Farm Road. The tree was blown down due to gusty winds along outflow from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough that neared the region on 7/9 entered the CWA on the 7/10, acting as the main forcing for convection during the afternoon and evening. The overall environment was similar to 7/9, with weak shear but moderate to strong instability and high PWAT's. This led to a severe thunderstorm in Orangeburg SC that caused tree damage and some roof damage to multiple buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reports roof damage to multiple buildings at 1098 Joe S Jeffords  Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough developed through the day, triggering scattered thunderstorm. Due to weak bulk shear and mixed layer CAPE over 3000 J/kg, just an isolated storm was able to reach severe limits and produce heavy rainfall during the day. In addition, light steering flow allowed the storm to move slow enough to produce flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bamberg County dispatch reported flash flooding along Heritage Hwy at Carolina Hwy in Denmark, SC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough developed through the day, triggering scattered thunderstorm. Due to weak bulk shear and mixed layer CAPE over 3000 J/kg, just an isolated storm was able to reach severe limits and produce heavy rainfall during the day. In addition, light steering flow allowed the storm to move slow enough to produce flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bamberg County Dispatch reported trees down on a powerline in Denmark, SC. Time  estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave rotating around the eastern side of a mid level ridge provided a lifting mechanism for widespread thunderstorms. CAPE values around 2000 J/kg was sufficient enough to produce several strong storms, but a lack of bulk shear kept all but one storm below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aiken County EM reported a tree fell on a car and destroyed it on Jackson Ave. Time  estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved southwest across the area around the southeast side of an upper level high in near record heat for June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was reported down on SC-51 near Hemingway Lane. A car crashed into the tree a short time  later - no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough in the region the past couple days began its exit on 7/11, but some weak shortwave energy coupled with weak surface troughing led to scattered convective development during the afternoon and evening.  MLCAPE in excess of 2500 J/kg developed by the late afternoon, though deep layer shear was under 20 kts. Steeper low level lapse rates brought high DCAPE values and thus this environment brought two severe thunderstorms, one in Lexington County and one in Orangeburg County, both causing tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lexington County EM reported a couple of trees down near Swansea blocking the  roadway. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge over the Southeast led to very warm temperatures. Additionally, localized surface convergence allowed isolated convection to occur.  High precipitable values over 2 inches caused heavy rainfall with the convection, resulting in an isolated instance of flash flooding in Leesville, SC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported flash flooding along Hwy 1 with water getting into a restaurant near Crouch St. Water was also approaching the front doors of businesses on Main St in Leesville.

The lightning strike started a fire which damaged electrical wiring in the attic and burned across several rafters. One occupant was treated for smoke inhalation but did not need to be transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours across southeast South Carolina. These thunderstorms produced frequent cloud to ground lightning, with one strike causing significant damage to a home in Colleton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colleton County Fire and Rescue reported that a home at 1462 Johnsville Road was heavily damaged by a fire due to electrical damage caused by a lightning strike. The home suffered significant damage due to the fire, especially on the back side of the structure. The occupant of the home suffered burns to the arms in attempts to extinguish the fire prior to the arrival of Fire and Rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous clusters of thunderstorms started developing across southeast South Carolina in the early afternoon hours and gradually spread eastward through the late afternoon. These storms produced damaging wind gusts, frequent cloud to ground lightning, heavy rainfall, and even a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Berkeley County Emergency Management reported that a lightning strike caused a house fire at a home on Hydra Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough in the region the past couple days began its exit on 7/11, but some weak shortwave energy coupled with weak surface troughing led to scattered convective development during the afternoon and evening.  MLCAPE in excess of 2500 J/kg developed by the late afternoon, though deep layer shear was under 20 kts. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave along the apex of an upper ridge and some weak surface troughing brought isolated convection across the Midlands in SC. Instability was modest with weak organizing shear, but by the late afternoon moderate DCAPE was seen in the central Midlands as deep mixing occurred. This led to a severe thunderstorm in southern Kershaw County that caused tree damage. There was also a reported 2-alarm fire in Richland County due to a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbia-Richland Fire Department reported responding to a 2-alarm fire caused by a  lightning strike in the 1100 block of  Bradford Ridge Lane. Two homes were left  uninhabitable, with a third sustaining minor damage. All occupants escaped with no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous clusters of thunderstorms started developing across southeast South Carolina in the early afternoon hours and gradually spread eastward through the late afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team confirmed an EF-0 tornado within the Savannah National Wildlife Refuge in Jasper County. The tornado touched down just east of Plantation Island Trail where it snapped some small tree limbs and the tops of some trees. The tornado moved toward the north-northwest, snapping more small tree limbs, before crossing Laurel Hill Wildlife Drive and ending just south of Highway 170.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak upper ridging along with a developing low off the Southeast coast allowed deep moisture to reach the eastern Midlands by the late afternoon on the 4th with PWAT's that reached 1.6-1.7 inches. This ample moisture and weak forcing/shear allowed a strong pulse environment to develop with MLCAPE approaching 1500-1800 J/kg by the afternoon. This led to the development of an isolated severe storm in Sumter County with reports of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SCDPS website reported a tree in roadway at the intersection of Church Street and West  Hampton Lane. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into the Southeast, acting as the main forcing in a strongly unstable environment with weak deep layer shear and high PWAT's. This forcing led to the development of scattered strong to severe convection that moved slowly due to the weak steering flow. The slow moving nature and efficient rainfall from these storms led to an area of flash flooding in Lancaster County, SC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lancaster County EM reported Oak Ridge Rd completely flooded. EM also reported that W Doc Garris Rd near Ridgewood Acres Rd was completely washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough that neared the region on 7/9 entered the CWA on the 7/10, acting as the main forcing for convection during the afternoon and evening. This led to a severe thunderstorm in Orangeburg SC that caused tree damage and some roof damage to multiple buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds resulting from high pressure building inland and developing low pressure offshore along with astronomical influences associated with a recent full moon and perigee led to coastal flooding along the South Carolina coast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing cold front, cold temperatures and elevated northwest winds combined to produce dangerously cold wind chill temperatures across southeast South Carolina. A few sites measured wind chill values as low as 20 degrees which meets the local criteria for a Cold Weather Advisory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) site at the Charleston International Airport (KCHS) measured a minimum wind chill value of 20 degrees (F).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rare November supercells developed across South Carolina as a deep mid and upper level trough pushed across the southeast US. This trough interacted with anomalously high instability and a steep moisture gradient that helped enhance the wind fields to favor supercellular convection. Each of these supercells produced large hail and damaging winds during their lifetime in the central and western Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McCormick County Dispatch reported powerlines down along New Cut Rd. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass became supportive of wet microbursts during the peak heating of the afternoon.  The instability that developed also benefitted from some weak shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was shared on social media of a tree down across  Calder Road between Marion and Mullins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A digging trough with strong mid-level winds helped trigger and develop a well organized mesoscale convective system across the southeast US on June 7th. Robust low level moisture and dry air aloft produced an ideal environment for strong instability to mix down strong winds to the surface. This resulted in a wide and long lived swath of damaging straight line winds throughout western and central SC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video posted on social media of numerous trees down along I20 near the bridge over the Broad River on the Richland County side. Trees were blocking the right two lanes of traffic. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early afternoon hours along a cold front and near a weak area of low pressure. These storms moved slowly across the Charleston metro area through the mid afternoon and produced very heavy rainfall. The excessive rainfall resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across the Charleston peninsula as well as in the West Ashley area. Observed rainfall amounts from CoCoRaHS observers and automated rain gages ranged from 1.5-4.0 inches for the entire event, including observed hourly rainfall rates of 2-3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charleston Police Department reported that the intersection of Rutledge Avenue and Simons Street was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread pulse thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across the Midlands, including isolated flash flooding in the Columbia metro in the evening of September 20th. Above average precipitable water, a weak approaching trough, and little wind shear produced slow moving storms with highly efficient rain rates of 3-4 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USGS Rocky Branch Creek stream gauge measured a stream level rising above flood  stage at 7.23 ft at 15:10, cresting at 7.57 ft at 15:24, and falling below flood stage (7.2 ft) at 15:31.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: RCWINDs rain gauge measured 1.34 inches of rain in 30 min.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel to quarter size hail near the Lake Bowen dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog formed in coastal South Carolina. Several accidents were reported on bridges over waterways. One victim was transported to the hospital, their condition unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle accidents reported on 544 bridge over the Intracoastal Waterway in Socastee. One victim had to be transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and storm clusters developed near the Blue Ridge during the afternoon and moved steadily southeast across the Upstate during the afternoon into the evening. Isolated severe storms produced locally damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported trees blown down at South Radio Station Rd and Pruitt Rd and at West Cherry Rd and Evening Shade Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching mid-level trough provided sufficient lift across the region to produce some scattered strong-severe thunderstorms across the western and eastern SC midlands. Some modest wind shear thanks to the trough along with moderate instability helped produce a broken line of strong storms. While most reports were sub-severe, the strongest and most organized thunderstorm produced isolated tree and power line damage in Harbison State Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SCDPS reported trees and power lines down in the road near Monticello Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and storm clusters developed near the Blue Ridge during the afternoon and moved steadily southeast across the Upstate during the afternoon into the evening. Isolated severe storms produced locally damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported up to quarter size hail along Hudson St in Taylors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same slow moving long wave trough caused severe weather on preceding days helped trigger and organize another broken line of severe convection in the Midlands of SC on June 9th. Sufficient low level shear and strong instability helped produce two tornadoes along with a swath of scattered damaging winds into the central Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbia news affiliate posted a picture from a viewer in Chapin of a large branch  snapped off a tree. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same slow moving long wave trough caused severe weather on preceding days helped trigger and organize another broken line of severe convection in the Midlands of SC on June 9th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: SCDPS website and Richland County EM reported a tree on the roadway on Lorick  Road near Dozier Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of very slow moving thunderstorms formed across eastern Lexington county, producing several areas of impactful flash flooding in West Columbia. With anomalously high precipitable water and weak shear, the cluster of storms moved very slowly over the course of several hours and dropped between 2-4 inches of rain in a highly urban area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle reported to be submerged in flood waters at the intersection of Sunset Blvd and Raleigh St. Water was getting inside the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe storms developed over central GA and moved northeastward into a favorable and very unstable environment characterized by CAPE in excess of 3000 J/kg with precipitable water values around 2 inches. Storms grew upscale and developed a strong cold pool and resulted in damaging wind gusts across the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Highway Patrol reported trees down along Highway 391 south of Prosperity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early afternoon hours along a cold front and near a weak area of low pressure. These storms moved slowly across the Charleston metro area through the mid afternoon and produced very heavy rainfall. The excessive rainfall resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across the Charleston peninsula as well as in the West Ashley area. Observed rainfall amounts from CoCoRaHS observers and automated rain gages ranged from 1.5-4.0 inches for the entire event, including observed hourly rainfall rates of 2-3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charleston Police Department reported that Washington Street was closed between Laurens Street and Society Street due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved into upstate South Carolina during the evening. A couple of the storms produced brief damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported (via Social Media) trees blown down southeast of Ware Shoals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved into upstate South Carolina from north Georgia and moved quickly across the area during the early evening. The storms produced multiple areas of damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported numerous trees, large limbs, and some power lines blown down throughout Anderson County. The ASOS at the Anderson Regional Airport measured a wind gust of 51 kts, or 60 mph.
